coredata.dc:title: 2D material-driven biosensors for heavy metal monitoring across the environment: Progress, challenges, and future directions

coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal ions (HMIs) accumulation beyond permissible limits is imposing risks to the environment. Accompanied by an array of challenges, conventional heavy metal ions (HMIs) monitoring techniques, such as spectrometry, are being discouraged for large-scale applications. This has instigated the research community to develop highly efficient HMI detection techniques with the motive of overcoming the drawbacks of conventional methodologies and developing user-friendly, cost-effective, highly efficient, and portable monitoring systems. Advancements in functional nanomaterials and selectively engineered nanostructures are paving the way for developing high-end biosensors for the detection of pollutants across the ecosystems. Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials (NMs) with unique structural attributes such as enlarged surface area, multiple layering and porosity, and ability to generate functionalities for targeted identification on their surface. Besides, exhibiting a great tendency to detect and respond to external stimuli. These features confer 2D materials to attune the material’s properties. Hence, the current article has highlighted the two-dimensional (2D) materials-based biosensing mechanism as it is a promising solution for the environmental remediation problem while addressing the challenges of traditional practices. Highly superior and sensing-oriented properties of 2D materials have been discussed with subsequent emphasis on their functionalization and various fabrication techniques for developing 2D materials-empowered biosensors, particularly essential and efficient for HMI detection across different ecosystems. Further, challenges associated with 2D-based biosensors and their effective suggested measures have been spotlighted. In the end, the integration of 2D materials-based biosensing techniques with emerging and innovative techniques such as modern nanofabrication processes, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), etc., has been featured for further advancements in the field of environmental remediation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal ions (HMIs) accumulation beyond permissible limits is imposing risks to the environment. Accompanied by an array of challenges, conventional heavy metal ions (HMIs) monitoring techniques, such as spectrometry, are being discouraged for large-scale applications. This has instigated the research community to develop highly efficient HMI detection techniques with the motive of overcoming the drawbacks of conventional methodologies and developing user-friendly, cost-effective, highly efficient, and portable monitoring systems. Advancements in functional nanomaterials and selectively engineered nanostructures are paving the way for developing high-end biosensors for the detection of pollutants across the ecosystems. Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials (NMs) with unique structural attributes such as enlarged surface area, multiple layering and porosity, and ability to generate functionalities for targeted identification on their surface. Besides, exhibiting a great tendency to detect and respond to external stimuli. These features confer 2D materials to attune the material’s properties. Hence, the current article has highlighted the two-dimensional (2D) materials-based biosensing mechanism as it is a promising solution for the environmental remediation problem while addressing the challenges of traditional practices. Highly superior and sensing-oriented properties of 2D materials have been discussed with subsequent emphasis on their functionalization and various fabrication techniques for developing 2D materials-empowered biosensors, particularly essential and efficient for HMI detection across different ecosystems. Further, challenges associated with 2D-based biosensors and their effective suggested measures have been spotlighted. In the end, the integration of 2D materials-based biosensing techniques with emerging and innovative techniques such as modern nanofabrication processes, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), etc., has been featured for further advancements in the field of environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overview of environmental contamination by heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: Atmosphere (air), lithosphere (land), and hydrosphere (water) together constitute the environment where living beings, including plants, animals, and micro-organisms, thrive. Any chemical or material present beyond the permissible limits in the environment and imposing deleterious impacts is categorised as a pollutant [1]. Sudden transformation in economic inputs from agriculture and handicrafts to the industrial revolution imparted prodigious consequences, which brought into limelight by Rachel Carson on September 27, 1962, in his publication entitled “Silent Spring” [2]. She unveiled the ambiguity behind the anthropogenic activities, viz., discharging domestic and industrial waste as well as chemically-derived synthetic agricultural raw material and its deleterious impact on the environment. The deleterious impact of these components on the environment, which was customarily categorised as anthropogenic activities, was raised by the deposition of domestic, industrial, and artificial agricultural inputs. Moreover, the anthropogenic activities emerging from industrial, agricultural, and urbanisation have significantly induced contamination in the rhizosphere and atmosphere, which involves the accumulation of heavy metals and various other obnoxious fumigant chemicals, imposing a threat to the environment [3–5]. With the Industrial Revolution, there is a significant accumulation of toxic heavy metals and other chemicals in the environment, besides agricultural activities and urbanisation [6]. At present, heavy metal (HM) contamination has become a critical issue worldwide. Excessive accumulation of HMs in any component of the ecosystem, viz., soil, water, and land, poses an immense risk to the environment as well as the living organisms surrounding it [7].

Nearly 20 million hectares of land, mainly agricultural land, is severely impacted by HM exposure, including chromium (Cr), nickel (Ni), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), arsenic (As), zinc (Zn), mercury (Hg) [8]. Heavy metals beyond permissible limits in the water bodies threaten the life of aquatic flora and fauna as these pollutants are non-biodegradable and persist in the medium for several years [9]. Besides, polluted water supply to urban areas interferes with the drinking water purity, which subsequently threatens the lives of living beings by attacking their vital organs [10]. Likewise, HM’s polluted air, when inhaled by individuals, leads to severe illness, particularly in children and immunocompromised people. In addition, the polluted air tarnishes the materials and buildings, thus declining the aesthetics and markedly elevating the maintenance cost. In general, environmental contamination can be classified into primary contamination, induced by the contaminants present near their source of origin; secondary contamination, instigated by the contaminants dispersed from their site of origin to other areas through air, water or soil; tertiary contamination, this refers to mobilization of pollutants including trace elements [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Importance of monitoring heavy metals across ecosystems
ce:section.ce:para: It is imperative to monitor heavy metals in different ecosystems because of their life-supporting, ecological, and societal importance. For instance, the agricultural ecosystem lays the foundation for human survival via nurturing and safeguarding it [12]. But, HM’s incorporation into the agricultural lands drastically reduces the soil nutrients, influencing crop yield and quality. Additionally, the immense use of agrochemicals, including pesticides and fertilisers, as well as absurd water usage for irrigation purposes, elevates agricultural soil pollution immensely, inducing ecological imbalance. Hence, soil monitoring for detecting early signs of HM contamination can be beneficial in implementing preventive measures before major devastation to the ecosystem [13]. Likewise, HM accumulation in the aquatic environments, including rivers, poses serious threats to living beings due to the phenomenon of bioaccumulation, biomagnification and dissipation of HM across the various trophic levels of the food chain. The HM contaminated river water is the major source of water supply for industrial, domestic and agricultural purposes. Thus, monitoring physicochemical attributes and content of HMs in the aquatic ecosystem is of prime importance as it possesses the potential to contaminate various other ecosystems as well as deteriorate human health [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of biosensors in environmental monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental protection from hazardous pollutants is a topmost concern worldwide due to their unavoidable interference in various aspects of life, creating a menace for all living entities [15]. Ongoing intensive research focuses on developing persistent environmental monitoring techniques precisely appropriate to manage hazardous pollutants fundamental to remediation strategies. The existing customary spectroscopic and chromatographic techniques developed to detect pollutants with great precision and sensitivity are usually refrained from usage as they are strenuous, involve toxic chemicals, time-inefficient, require multiple sample preparation steps, and require expert technicians to operate them. Thus, tackling the issues linked with classical pollutant detection techniques led to the discovery of advanced biosensing technologies, which promised to deliver high selectivity, sensitivity, precision, quick action and real-time monitoring [16]. The past several decades seem to be an era of biosensors, as rapid progression was seen in developing diverse biosensors for the identification of an array of analytes, viz., fungi, bacteria, drugs, and food additives, besides hazardous pollutants [17]. Hence, they are of utmost importance in different fields, such as medicine, industry, agriculture, pharmaceutics, and environmental monitoring.

Biosensing is a biotechnology-based analytical technique for the detection of pollutants, comprising signal transducing elements which generate detectable signals on sensing any pollutant. Biosensors play a pivotal role in reducing the carbon footprint as their energy requirement for contaminant detection is very low, compared to conventional techniques. Hence, biosensors can be considered as an important environmental monitoring technique which contributes towards achieving sustainable development [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction to 2D materials and their relevance in biosensing
ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) materials refer to crystalline solids consisting of mono or multiple atomic layers. Owing unique structural organisation and chemical constitution, 2D materials have been classified as superconductors, semiconductors, insulators, metallic and semi-metallic [19]. Emergence of 2D materials established from graphene in 2014 to plumbene at present, with subsequent discovery of phosphorene, silicene, tellurene, antimonene, selinene, MXenes, transition metal oxides (TMOS), transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDS), hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), black phosphorus (BP), layered double hydroxides (LDH), etc. in between, has gained attention from diverse fields, particularly monitoring, detection and sensing [20–22]. In general, 2D materials have been categorised mainly into four types, viz., graphene family, Xenes family, chalcogenides family, and 2D oxides family ( Fig. 1).

The graphene family consists of graphene and its derivatives, represented in Fig. 1A (a-g), possessing diverse hybridised carbon (C) atoms or mixed elements. Allotropes of C, viz., graphyne, are represented by acetylene chains instead of C-C bonds in case of graphene. Further, replacing original C atoms with h-BN, boron-carbon-nitrogen (BCN), and Sixc1-x forming more complex hexagonal layered structures that are analogous to graphene. Moreover, the tetragonal arrangement of 2D material also exhibits greater stability, as seen in the case of titanium carbide (TiC). Xenes’ family include 2D materials with a single element structured into contorted trigonal or hexagonal lattices. They include group IIIA, IVA, and VA elements viz., geranium (Ge), silicon (Si), tin (Sn), antimony (Sb), and boron (B) respectively termed as germanene, silicene, stanine, phosphorene, and antimonene, borophene [Fig. 1B(a-c)]. Xenes group possess characteristic alternate out-of-plane atomic arrangements forming anisotropic lattice assembly, different from the typical planar atomic arrangement in graphene. Chalcogenides family comprises of emerging 2D materials incorporating transitional metal dichalcogenides (TMDS) MX2, M: molybdenum (Mo), tungsten (W), niobium (Nb), tantalum (Ta), represents layer transitional metal atom which is entrapped from both sides with two layers of chalcogen atoms presented as X include sulphur (S), selenium (Se), tellurium (Te) [Fig. 1C(a-d)]. The 2D Oxides family comprises phosphorus (P), lead (Pb) and transition metal oxides [Fig. 1D (a-d)]. These are usually found as single planar, multilayer, and superlattice arrangements. Layered 2D metal oxides possess robust lateral bonding in planes and fragile van der Waals forces in between layers, whereas non-layered 2D oxides are capable of exhibiting atomic interaction in all three dimensions [23].

The emerging 2D materials including MXenes, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), manganese dioxide (MnO2), metal organic frameworks (MOFs), graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) possess highly tunable band gaps in between the insulator and semiconductor phases, hence surpassing current leakage and conferring superior sensitivity. This has driven an intense wave towards biosensor fabrication based upon 2D materials for the detection of many biological molecules as well as reactions. These emerging 2D materials are playing outstanding roles in field-effect transistors (FET) owing to their magnificent electronic, optical, and chemical attributes [24]. The 2D materials-derived FET biosensors play pivotal role in diverse fields such as environmental monitoring including detection of organic and inorganic pollutants from different ecosystems, biosecurity applications via sensing and identifying infectious components, hazardous materials, biological threats for conferring healthy and safe environment to the living organisms, and identifying human lifestyle disorders such as diabetes via accurate and real time detection of biological targets viz., serum, sweat, saliva, and urine [25]. Besides, 2D materials have been used to fabricate photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensors without involving conventional metallic semiconductors. For instance, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) exhibit high potential in developing PEC biosensors as reported by Liu and co-workers for the detection of chloramphenicol [26]. Two-dimensional NMs give an ideal platform to load fluorescence probes due to their extremely large surface area and outstanding biocompatibility for in situ image detection and analysis of biological molecules. In this regard, Liu and co-workers used GO coupled with catalysed hairpin assembly for developing a nanoprobe to conduct image analysis of cellular mirna. Based upon this amplification process, they were able to visualise mirna image analysis even at minute concentrations in MCF-10 cells [27].

MXenes-derived electrochemical biosensors give promising results for the identification of cancer biomarkers with high sensitivity and reliability, conferring great potential in early-stage diagnostic techniques. For well in advance diagnosis of tumour cells, expression of folate receptor (FR) plays a major role as it is a critical biomarker to recognise tumour-related pathologies. Hence, quantifying it with great precision can support early diagnosis of cancerous cells, and based on that, therapeutic strategies can be developed in a minimal time duration. The electrochemical biosensors integrated with advanced electrode materials for sensitivity and detection optimisation can play a crucial role. In this regard, Jiang and co-workers fabricated extremely stable and highly conductive nanocomposite material by in situ coupling of MXene and first-generation poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimers, synthesising highly powerful immobilisation matrix viz., PAMAM@MXene. This composite material is then integrated onto screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCES) for developing a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor to detect FR. It is observed that the developed sensor displayed outstanding output under optimised conditions with a wide quantification range of nearly 10–1000 ng/mL for FR with a LOD of 5.6 ng/mL. Besides, it also exhibited significant reproducibility, sensitivity, and stability, indicating its high potential in clinical application for advancing tumour diagnostic techniques [28].

MXene-based electrochemical biosensors also display a significant impact on the detection of chemical messengers such as serotonin (5-HT). This is a crucial chemical messenger as it regulates various physiological processes and associated psychiatric and neurological disorders in the human body. For the detection of this neurotransmitter, a scientific group developed a novel electrochemical biosensor using a nanocomposite-modified SPCE synthesised by integration of MXene with 2D FeVO4 nanoflakes, namely FeVO4@Ti3C2 MXene-modified SPCE. It is seen that the biosensor displayed a linear detection range of nearly 25–75 nM with a detection limit of 5.88 nM and sensitivity in the range of nearly 3.03.0 μA μM−1cm−2. Moreover, this biosensor exhibited high output in real sample analysis as well such as human serum, indicating its importance in diagnostic and pharmaceutical applications [29], [30].

The relevance of 2D materials in sensing technology is reflected in their certain properties. The most appropriate characteristic of 2D materials is an astonishingly high surface-area-to-volume ratio due to single-layered structure with a thickness of up to a few nanometres. This confers 2D materials with an ample number of reactive junctions for analytes to be recognised. Moreover, conductivity in the case of 2D materials is tunable, achieved either through managing structural deformities, enhancing layer count, doping or their post-functionalization. Further, the mechanical durability and pliability of 2D materials make them highly congenial with futuristic technologies, viz., printing methods, ultrathin silicon channels, flexible and wearable electronics, as well as metal electrodes [31]. The significant contribution of various 2D nanomaterials such as graphene, MXenes, TMDS, BP, and h-BN in the field of biosensing reported in the last decade is evident from the high number of research publications linked with it obtained from Scopus using keywords: graphene, MXenes, TMDS, BP, h-BN, biosensors, biosensing, as mentioned in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of 2D material-driven biosensors and their potential impact on environmental monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The unique physicochemical properties of 2D NMs support environmental pollution monitoring through innovative biosensing techniques. The superior electrical conductivity and facile graphene oxide (GO) functionalization facilitate HM-sensitive enzyme’s immobilisation on transistor interfaces for developing spontaneous electrochemical biosensors relevant for monitoring water-based pollutants [32]. Moreover, gas sensors based upon 2D materials provides valuable tool for monitoring the air quality index as it is highly efficient in detecting the obnoxious gases such as sulphur dioxide (SO2), hydrogen sulphide (H2S), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and various volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [33], [34]. Different studies evidence the effective role of 2D NMs-derived electrochemical sensors for detecting even minute concentrations of these gases owing to their enlarged surface area and high conductivity, facilitating quick electron transfer and superior target analytes interaction [35], [36]. Graphene-induced photoplasma nanoscale cavities have been proposed by Yin and co-workers for the detection of the photodegradation process. It is based upon the principle of real-time monitoring of the organic dye degradation kinetics exposed to a light source, at the molecular scale, using a graphene-activated photoplasma chamber. They monitored the photodegradation process of the Rhodamine 6 G molecule occurring on graphene-activated sensor using optical resonance displacement evaluation and laser local illumination [37]. The semiconducting transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDS) possess high potential for optical identification of fertilisers and pesticides in aqueous bodies through fluorescence quenching or using antibodies labelled with TMDS for Forster resonance energy transfer [38]. Besides, field-deployed bioassays using graphene quantum dots (GQDS) have been applied for pathogenic microbe detection from potable aqueous bodies as well as recreational water sources through fluorescence labelling technologies [39].

Titanium oxide (TiO2) nanostructures play an important role in the gas sensing mechanism for the detection of hazardous gaseous molecules from the environment. However, pristine TiO2 NMs exhibit limitations due to a large band gap, which gives poor response and low selectivity of TiO2-derived gas sensors. Hence, to address this challenge, TiO2 NMs have been functionalized using noble metals (such as gold, silver, platinum etc,), doped with elements (such as manganese, nickel, cobalt, iron, etc.), constructing heterojunctions (such as n-n type, p-n type, and p-p type), and compounding them with other functional materials (such as nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, transition metal dichalcogenides, MXenes, etc.), developing multi-component composite NMs based upon TiO2. Owing to these modifications, TiO2-based gas sensors exhibited high selectivity, sensitivity, and subsided operating temperature due to enhancement in its conductivity, superior surface area, improved adsorption and catalytic properties, as compared to pristine TiO2 [40].

To identify the role of 2D materials in environmental remediation applications in general, the author keywords ( Table 1) derived from every single publication obtained via Scopus were analysed using VOSviewer. Keywords mentioned by the authors portray the core idea of their research. Hence, bibliometric analysis of the keywords provided by the authors in all the publications related to a particular field displays the major hotspots of that research field. Fig. 3(a) indicates the output of co-occurrence analysis of author keywords occurring at least with a frequency above 5. The size of the node is dependent upon the occurrence frequency. The bigger the node higher the occurrence frequency. It is observed that though 2D materials have been utilised extensively for environmental remediation applications as photocatalytic agents and adsorbents, there is a big lag in the research documents depicting their role in biosensing applications for environmental clean-up, as the author keyword “sensor” has an occurrence frequency of 5 only (Table 1). Besides, the pie chart in Fig. 4 represents that only 33 scientific documents were obtained from Scopus in the last decade (more precisely from 2017 to 2024) with keywords “2D materials based biosensors for environmental monitoring”, indicating a minimally explored aspect of 2D materials in environmental remediation applications. As mentioned above, 2D materials exhibit marvellous properties being prioritised for developing biosensors, thus this review article has been planned to represent the biosensing properties of 2D materials in connection with their importance for environmental monitoring, to fill the gap being overlooked by the technologists, environmentalists and research community. Fig. 3(b), representing another aspect of Fig. 2(a), gives an overview of the chronological order of keyword formation. The terms indicated in purple or dark blue colour represent their average year of publication on or before 2020, whereas current terms are indicated in yellow and green colour.

Materials possessing piezoelectric properties are often polar and pivotal for actuators. Precise manipulations can be induced in such materials by applying a voltage, causing minute physical deformations [41]. Integration of innovative equipment, viz. scanning tunnelling microscope (STM), makes piezoelectric actuators a wonderful tool for accomplishing outstanding accuracy positioning. Besides gaining success in exploring microworld, sub-atomic localisation with an extreme precision is still a challenging task. In comparison to conventional bulk materials, 2D materials exhibit marvellous piezoelectric characteristics owned through vanished central symmetry as main segments in case of 2D materials are compacted to a single atomic layer [42], [43]. Moreover, heteroatom addition further improves the piezoelectric properties of 2D materials. The utility of 2D materials in the piezoelectric equipment depends significantly upon their strain behaviour. The alterations in the bond lengths induce deformations in the dimensions of 2D materials, thus, the strain produced in ion complexation is harnessed for converting electrical energy into mechanical energy.

According to the Peltier effect and the Seebeck effect, thermoelectric materials are represented by unique materials capable of interconverting heat and electric energy [44]. The extraordinary features, viz., noise proof, eco-friendly and low maintenance, have attracted worldwide attention [45]. As compared to conventional organic solid materials, low-dimensional materials possess a greater ability for efficient thermoelectric conversions. It is observed that 2D materials possess eccentric thermal transport features attained by distinct phonon dispersion association, when compared to their bulk counterparts [46]. To understand, the bulk structure displays linear dispersion interconnection of acoustic phonons, and 2D materials portray parabolic form, in the long-wave estimation, diminishing the phonons’ velocity and impacting their scattering pattern, which ultimately affects heat transfer characteristics of materials [47]. Moreover, declining dimensions of 2D materials also involve the effect of the interface on thermal conduction, which prominently enhances their performance. Contrastingly, polymer matrices exhibit robust phonon interface scattering induced by the layered interface, subsequently reducing their thermal conductivity. Due to the quantum confinement effect, 2D materials possess a high Seebeck coefficient. As the electron mass elevates abruptly around the Fermi level, the net cross sections of low-dimensional materials tend to disperse phonons significantly [48], [49].

The electronic structure configuration and exhibiting internal properties of 2D functional materials are correlated. The alterations in the structural arrangement of repeating sub-units and layering pattern between the layers bring potential changes in the electrical and electrochemical attributes of 2D materials. The unique electronic distribution features of 2D materials classify them as an excellent components for electrochemical devices. The promising role of 2D materials in electrochemical devices is attributed to their structural flexibility, which can be modified to bring optimisation in the electrochemical performance of devices. These modifications include varying covalent bonding surroundings, inculcating either a single metal atom of atomic scale or metal clusters of nanoscale via non-covalent interactions [50].

Graphene is labelled as a key member in the world of 2D materials, which predominates the leading and innovative technologies such as novel electronics and sensor technology. Despite being the smallest material in the universe, it is the strongest one. Graphene comprises of mono-layer honeycomb crystalline structure with SP2 hybridised C atoms appearing as a membrane-like structure. The C atom possesses six electrons, four of which are present in the valence shell. Amongst them, three electrons are linked with the other three carbon atoms in the hexagonal 2D dimension, and the remaining electrons move freely in the 3D space below and above the GR structure, which speeds up the mobility as compared to other materials. Sometimes, these π electrons, owing to quantum characteristics, exhibit zero mass, thus capable of conferring higher conductivity and electronic attributes to graphene when compared with conventional materials. It is characterised by sturdy optical non-linearity, outstanding quantum output facilitating light-matter interactions, and possessing photons with defined characteristics [51]. Various studies have explored the electrical attributes of graphene, which are exceptionally superior and unique, to numerous other condensed matter components. It has been observed that the optimisation of electronic and optical characteristics of bulk materials requires their thinner and smaller versions to tune their bandgap. Hence, 2D materials, including graphene with ultrathin monolayer structure, provide an important platform to open the bandgap, which otherwise is impossible to attain in bulk materials [52]. The fabrication of graphene also leads to the synthesis of its derivatives, viz., graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), with structural deformity and possessing oxygen-based surface functional groups [53]. The oxygen-based functional groups modulate the π electrons. Introduction of functional group disrupts the normal SP2 arrangement of graphene by altering the π integrated network; however, it manipulates the electronic band structure and density in GO and alters its electronic attributes by establishing localised electronic states inside the bandgap. The pure graphene possesses zero bandgap, which differentiates it from GO, in which a bandgap appears due to the addition of oxygen-based functional groups. The bandgap modifications are an important aspect for the field of optoelectronics and electronics, which is influenced significantly by the type and amount of functionalization of the 2D material [54].

Another class of 2D materials, i.e., TMDS, possess a planar structure similar to graphene and superior in fluorescence dispersion and quenching, outrageous electronic mobility, great surface-to-volume proportion, marked compatibility with advanced fabrication techniques, and most preferred replacement for graphene and its derivatives. Besides, covalent bonding between X-M-X (where X represents chalcogen and M represents transition metal) forming layer, there exists van der Waals interaction between these layers, promoting layer-managed fabrication process [55]. Almost all TMDS exhibit excellent semiconducting electrical characteristics, unlike graphene, thus suitable for their usage in developing integrated electronic circuits [56], [57]. Extraordinary optoelectronic attributes are executed by TMDS when monolayers are formed from their multilayer structure. Moreover, there is a shift in the electronic band structure from indirect to direct, exhibiting robust photoluminescence and powerful excitation binding force [58]. The ultra-thin dimensions of TMDS, along with an extensive surface-to-volume ratio, make them highly suitable for anchoring a large number of biomolecules on them, thus tremendously increasing selectivity and sensitivity with subsequent reduction in biosensor power utilisation [59]. Moreover, amendments in the structure of TMDS through advanced innovative fabrication techniques further improve their selectivity, i.e., ability to detect particular analytes amongst similar types of analytes [60]. Thus, layer-derived tunable band structure, minute toxicity, higher biocompatibility, besides above mentioned characteristics of TMDS, are inducing a lot of interest for developing next-generation biosensing devices for applications in diverse fields.

MXenes are a new class of 2D materials discovered for the first time by Gogotsi and co-workers in 2011. The term MXenes is derived from MAX phase, which is the parent material in MXene fabrication. The MAX phase consists of tightly aligned multi-layers (Mn+1AXn) of ternary nitrides and carbides in which M and A are linked with powerful M-X covalent bonds [61]. MXenes are derived from MAX phase through the process of etching, applying hydrofluoric (HF) solution as an etchant and represented as Mn+1XnTx, where n varies from 1 to 3 and Tx presents surface functional groups such as =O, -OH, -F, -Cl, etc. [62]. MXenes are being explored by scientists and material engineers due to their magnificent mechanical, electronic, and electrical properties, and their electronic and dimensional attributes further shape their optical properties. A better optical output can be generated at a particular wavelength via band-to-band electronic transitions. It has been investigated through various scientific studies that the energy structure, viz., direct/indirect band gaps, energy bandgap, and topological insulators, as well as the dielectric constant of the surrounding medium of MXenes, greatly impact their linear and non-linear optical attributes. Moreover, the band gap can be modified by introducing different surface functional groups, more electronegative termination results into a broader band gap [63]. Besides, MXenes owe unique integration of different promising characteristics viz., well-versed redox-active sites, greater tendency of immobilising biomaterials, and accelerated transduction of optical and electrical signals. They possess the ability to bind many target-specific biomolecules, which subsequently adds to the specificity of sensors. The precise solution dispersibility feature of MXenes can be explored to develop electrodes for electrochemical sensors. Hence, outstanding optoelectronic characteristics of MXenes can revolutionise the world of sensing as they can greatly enhance the sensitivity as well as the limit of detection even with minute concentrations of the analyte [64].

Another emerging class of 2D materials which is gaining a lot of attention are 2D transition metal oxide nanomaterials, as they exhibit exceptionally important characteristics. They are rich in surface functionalization, have scalable electronic attributes, outstanding thermal and chemical stabilities, robust oxidation tendency, wide surface area, and marvellous optical characteristics. 2D TMONS can exist in diverse structural forms, including mono-planar structure, multi-layer or multi-layer of single transition metal oxide, and super lattice arrangement of discrete TMONS [65]. 2D TMONS exhibit different surface chemical characteristics as compared to their bulk counterparts, as the energy obtained from oxygen voids in 2D TMONS reduces prominently with subsequent enhancement in metal cations reduction. Hence, their redox attributes are reversible as well as unique, which makes them suitable for catalytic, electronic, optical, energy technology and biosensing applications [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of 2D materials in biosensing applications
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the previous data, it can be inferred that 2D materials are remarkable in the field of biosensing with lucrative advantages, as selectivity and sensitivity of the biosensing devices can be improved outrageously. The selectivity of 2D material-based biosensors is linked with the large surface-area-to-volume ratio as well as superior electron mobility [67]. Besides, ample active sites are responsible for outstanding electrocatalytic ability, which boosts the inbuilt currents with a simultaneous decrease in the redox potential while the analyte is attached. Moreover, 2D configuration confers them with incredible capacity for adsorbing gases with enhancement in their interaction with the surface [31]. Moreover, enhanced crystallinity along with raised conductivity keeps noise pollution under check. Besides, layered geometry as well as sub-nanometric voids in between the layers prompt extraordinary piezoelectric characteristics with magnified gauge factors. This significantly elevates the sensitivity of 2D material-derived biosensing devices with expanding their limit to identify even the weakest of the mechanical force [31]. Furthermore, the tendency of 2D materials to undergo effortless surface modifications or functionalization adds another feature highly favourable for their electronic rearrangement and desirable changes in their crystalline structure [68].
Various advantages and disadvantages of different classes of 2D materials for biosensing applications have been briefed in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of heavy metal detection biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Metals are indispensable as they are an integral part of a lot of vital biological processes, as well as industries of foremost importance. However, their accumulation in the environment beyond permissible limits is of great concern due to its negative impacts on human health and the ecosystem. Hence, necessary control measures are required for limiting their content and before that, highly precise detection techniques for sensing their presence and estimating their concentration are required. To fulfil this, time- and cost-effective detection methods have been recognised, which are discussed below.

The basic structure of an electrochemical sensor comprises three-electrode system, viz., working/sensing electrode (WE), counter electrode (CE), and reference electrode (RE) ( Fig. 5A). Modifications in the WE with different materials can enhance its detection precision and limit it tremendously. The underlying principle in electrochemical sensors involves output transducer signals and recognition of the potential difference by a potentiostat [69] (Fig. 5A).

Heavy metals are either reduced or oxidised by losing or gaining an electron on the surface of the sensing electrode; thereafter, chemical signals are transformed to electrical signals and the electrochemical detection assembly acquires the correlation between the induced current and HM’s content, thus prompting electrochemical measurement [73]. As mentioned, the major role is played by electrodes in electrochemical sensors, thus, their modification with emerging interface materials, particularly 2D materials such as graphene, MXenes, TMDS, metal oxide NPs, etc., can significantly enhance the reliability, sensitivity, subsequently delimiting detection time and concentration of HMs. For instance, arsenic (As) metal ions in drinking water have been detected with the fabrication of a modified electrochemical sensor. Iron oxide (Fe3 O 4) NPs were amalgamated with room temperature ionic liquid (RTIL), possessing high electrical conductivity as well as viscosity for elevating detection precision, to develop Fe3O4NPs-RTIL composite. Further, drop-casting this composite on a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) surface for electrode amendment, which possesses a better tendency to adsorb and detect As metal ions [72]. In another study, a composite was synthesized by integrating Fe3O4NPs and gold (Au) NPs for detection of As metal ions in drinking water and it was observed that this composite exhibited outstanding efficiency for entrapping As metal ions via Fe2+/Fe3+ cycle (Fig. 5B, C, and D), and marvellous stripping attributes for As metal ions, portraying great adsorption ability of Fe3O4NPs and catalytic activity of AuNPs [74].
There are different electrochemical sensing techniques for HM detection as mentioned below:

Potentiostatic techniques: In this method, a constant potential is maintained between the counter and reference electrodes by applying a potentiostat. Thus, by accounting for the potential, the particular analyte concentration can be determined. This is further subcategorised into amperometry, chronocoulometry, and voltammetry. In amperometry, non-mercuric electrodes are used for detecting minute currents at constant voltage. The current is determined and recorded as a function of time. In the case of chronocoulometry, after applying a tuned potential, the amount of charge generated is recorded after computing the internal current versus voltage or time. The major utility of this form of electrochemical sensing is for quantitative examination after conducting elaborate electrolysis, but it is not suitable for detecting the analyte type [75]. In voltammetry, current is measured at numerous potentials using the current voltage curve, contrary to the uniform potential in the case of amperometry. Due to greater precision and sensitivity, this technique is considered as most valuable tool amongst other electrochemical sensing techniques for detecting trace amounts of the HMs [76]. Moreover, modifications of electrodes with emerging materials have revolutionised the voltametric techniques by improving catalytic impact, reducing interruptions, thus flourishing a tenable alternative to electrochemical sensors [67] ( Fig. 6c).

Galvanostatic techniques: In this methodology, a source of current is applied to manage the electric current between the counter electrode and working electrode; simultaneously, the potential thus generated is recorded between them. Though the instrumentation in this electrostatic technique is much less complicated than compared of the potentiostatic technique, it is less prevalent due to certain drawbacks induced by extensive double-layer charging impact [75] (Fig. 6b).

Impedance measurement techniques: These involve AC voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), amongst which EIS is more frequently used impedance measuring technique for evaluating the analyte content, particularly linked to environmental and biological samples. This technique implies electrically equivalent circuits to express the products of an electrochemical process in an electrolytic cell. It is observed that both Faradic and non-Faradic constituents are generated due to charge movement via an electrified interface. Thus, the metal ion content is determined through this impedance criterion of the electric equivalent circuits [75] (Fig. 6d).

Electrochemiluminescence techniques: This methodology relies on the involvement of chemical reactions for measuring the analyte amount. The underlying principle is the conversion of data obtained with chemical reactions into detectable conductivity, current, potential or voltage. This sensing technique involves a chemically exclusive layer for perceiving electro-lysed chemical reactions and an electrochemical transducer for converting chemical alterations to electrical output. This electrochemical sensing technique promises to replace all the conventional methods of determining analytes, both qualitatively and quantitatively. These sensors have an edge over being highly responsive, precise, simple to use, easy to carry, and rapid processing [75] (Fig. 6a).

The major optical sensing mechanisms for heavy metal detection involve fluorescence and colourimetric detection mechanisms. A lot of research has been conducted in developing highly sensitive metal ion detection sensors based upon the principles of intramolecular charge transfer, electron movement, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer, as discussed below:

Photo-induced electron transfer mechanism (PET): The fluorescence detection of metal ions based upon the principle of PET involves the transfer of electrons between the electron donor and acceptor on light illumination, causing fluorescence exhaustion and sometimes even capturing of the fluorophore. On integrating the analyte with the sensor, there was a diminished PET effect, subsequently regaining fluorescent emission of the fluorophore. Hence, a sensor based on PET comprises of an electron donor possessing a receptor connecting via a spacer group to the fluorophore. The quenching of fluorescence happens due to the PET process, stimulated by excitation after light exposure, between the acceptor and fluorophore. However, the PET phenomenon is inhibited after binding of the analyte to the receptor, thus reestablishing fluorescence. This technique is highly precise, sensitive and efficient in HM ion detection [78] [ Fig. 7A (a)].

Intermolecular charge transfer (ICT): Based upon the ICT principle, a fluorescence sensing mechanism involves the shifting of electrons from the electron donor to the electron acceptor group on stimulation with light energy. The electron donor and receiver group in the case of the ICT-based sensor is affixed with a fluorescent group, resulting in a conjugated system [78]. There are two pathways in the determination of metal cations, viz., their collaboration with electron donor groups results in the development of coordination linkage with cations, instead of the usual transfer of π-pair electrons to the conjugated system, which causes a blue shift of the fluorescence spectrum. On the other hand, a red shift in the fluorescence spectrum was observed because of molecular charge relocation on the clubbing of metal ions with the electron donor groups [79] [Fig. 7A (b)].

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET): the fluorescence mechanism based upon this principle requires certain essential conditions, such as the gap between the energy giver and the receiver should be very less, there should be imposition between the dissipation spectrum of the energy giver and acceptor, the energy giver and acceptor must be aligned in a particular manner. This system consisted of two diverse fluorescent groups named energy donor (D) and energy acceptor (A). On a flash of excited wavelength of donor D, there is excitation in the fluorescence system, which may persuade transfer of non-radiative energy from D to A. FRET-based fluorescence sensors attach to an analyte via bonding elements, which alter the basic conditions, such as the gap and alignment between D and A. However, if these alterations remain within limits, there is the transfer of non-radiative energy from D to A, which further exhibits fluorescence amendments. On the contrary, if the basic conditions are not met on attaching with the analyte, then there is inhibition in the FRET phenomenon [78] [Fig. 7A (c)].

Optical fibre sensors are another class of optical sensing for the detection of HM ions. It consists of four major units, viz., source of light, fibre for transmittance, sensing zone and signal receiving end, generally enclosing spectrometers or photoelectric converters. Amongst these, fibre is the most important constituent through which light is transmitted to the signal receiving end via the sensing zone and covered by a cladding from outside. Fibres for HM ion detection sensors generally exhibit the principle of total internal reflection, which allows transmittance of light signals over longer distances without much loss of power [80]. For developing optical fibre biosensors, some portion of the cladding is replaced with the metal coating as well as biosensing element, including enzymes, antibodies, which react with the analytes to be detected [Fig. 7B (a & b)].

Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensors are one of the most extensively explored and applied biosensors as they are highly precise, sensitive and inexpensive. A typical SPR sensor is shown in Fig. 7C (a) in which incident light is reflected on the detector through a gold layer coated on the prism. The working mechanism of the SPR biosensor involves surface charge density vibrations of the free electrons at the metal-dielectric-ally interface [82]. A fugitive wave is generated on efficient excitation of electrons via a ray of incident light. This sensing mechanism requires a glass prism exhibiting a high refractive index, which coordinates the optical signals at the alloy of metal-dielectric. The alterations in refractive index at the detection area examined changes in the resonance angle that is used for biosensing activity [83], [84].

The piezoelectric biosensors refer to the biosensor assembly in which a recognition element is combined with a piezoelectric component, which works as a transducer. A lot of natural and artificial materials have been recognised to show piezoelectric effects, however, quartz crystals seem to be most frequently used material due to their extensive accessibility, thermal as well as chemical stability, respectively, at high temperatures and chemical environments in aqueous solution [85]. The piezoelectric effect can be explained as alterations in electric polarisation generated in some non-conductive materials on applying mechanical compression, and vice versa. It means, in reverse, application of an electric field induces deformation in these materials, which may induce compressive or tensile strain and stress as a result of the electric field direction. The supremacy linked with these types of transducers includes high frequency output, ease in usage, high flexibility, greater dielectric constant. However, certain drawbacks such as not preferred for detection under constant conditions, resulting output may deviate depending upon the temperature changes, which also depend upon crystal cut, the relative humidity also affects the output, which requires crystal layering with polymers or nanomaterials [85].

The FET biosensors consist of three electrodes, viz., source, gate and drain, a semiconductor layer, and an insulating layer. On achieving threshold voltage by the gate voltage, this device activates, and beyond threshold voltage, there is movement of carriers beside the channel between the drain and the source. The mechanism of these sensors involves movement of electrons from a semiconductor to a metal or vice versa due to work function differences, whether n semiconductor and a metal are nearby, thus creating a space charge area. Thereafter, the energy band corners of the semi-conductor are relocated consistently due to charge transfer facilitated electric field generation, termed as semiconductor/metal-contact-triggered band bending. Moreover, exposure to an externally supplied electric field to the metal builds an electric field between the semiconductor and the metal. Inadequate shielding effect by the charge carrier of minute concentration induces the inflow of electric field to the nearby areas of the semiconductor, commencing field-effect-instigated band bending [86].

This biosensing technique involves the induction of light signals through an electrochemical process. The ECL sensors comprise of a photomultiplier and an electrochemical workstation [87]. The underlying principle involves electrochemically induced redox reactions generating measurable light signals controlled by the electrode, which possess chemiluminescent material on their surface. ECL sensing technique has an edge over other conventional metal ion detection processes in having superior sensitivity, low operational cost and being highly portable [88], [89].

The proteins or their derivatives, as well as functional nucleic acids as bio-receptors for various sensing techniques, have shown promising outcomes for HM ions detection. Peptides refer to small chains of amino acids, whereas enzymes consist of different peptide chains linked to each other in various conformations. It is observed that peptides are preferred for designing high-end extremely precise biosensors due to their ability to sway metal-ligand interaction, and enzymes, being biological catalysts when integrated within the sensing architecture, promptly elevate their performance output [90]. Enzymes being used for biorecognition elements exhibit prompt, non-complicated and more precise detection of HM ions. The major drawbacks with enzyme-based recognition elements in biosensors are high maintenance cost, sensitivity of enzymes towards external environmental conditions, and multiple detection processes are very unstable [91].

Antibodies are protein-containing compounds, also referred to as immunoglobulins (Ig), that come into action during the immune response of the biological system against foreign bodies called antigens. They are fork (Y) shaped bodies which are utilised as bio-recognition components of the biosensors for identifying particular metal ions. The biosensors based upon antibody biorecognition elements are highly portable, easy to operate and inexpensive. Various antibodies, viz., monoclonal, polyclonal, and recombinant, are developed and clamped on the receptor. The introduction of an analyte leads to its attachment to a particular probe. The interaction between them is identified, and signals thus generated are worked upon by a transducer, which subsequently converts them into a readable form by applying a signal processing unit [92].

It is a decade-old practice wherein both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells are used in biosensors for the detection of HM ions. The cells include algae, fungi, protozoa, microorganisms, and plant cells. The mechanism involves the sensing of HM ions through metalloproteins, which act as a transcriptional factor which modulates the expression of downstream genes when biosensors are attached to the HM ions. The whole cell is generally obtained from culturing different strains of bacteria or other organisms, which, on exposure to HM ions, allow them to enter inside through the cell membranes. HM ions than starts interacting with the functional proteins via DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid), which ultimately synthesises reporter protein through the process of transcription and translation. The signals thus generated are received by the transducer, which transforms them into a readable form [92].

Due to the greater affinity of HM ions for DNA, demand for DNA-based biosensors has increased significantly in the last few years. The underlying mechanism involves the hybridisation of complementary strands of DNA. These are further categorised into genosensors and aptasensors. In the case of genosensors, immobilisation and selection of particular probes is important, whereas in aptasensors, the sequence of nucleic acid is selected randomly depending upon the high detection capability applying the Systematic Evolution of Ligands by Exponential Enrichment [93]. The aptamers have advantages as they are easy to develop, highly stable even in non-suitable environments, easy to store, compact, high shelf life, and are inexpensive with greater chemical stability. The overall mechanism of DNA-based biosensors involves the interaction of DNA with HM ions, which leads to the production of altered proteins. These changes are detected by the transducer, and with the application of a signal processing entity, the signals are converted to a readable format [92].

Fig. 8 portrays the various elements in the biosensors with only alterations at the biorecognition elements, viz., enzymes, antibodies, bacterial cells, and DNA, which are represented with their respective icons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and functionalization of 2D material-based biosensors

ce:section.ce:para: Modifications in the 2D nanomaterial’s structure significantly elevate their functionality and broaden their applicability in diverse fields. There are several modifications and functionalisation techniques for this class of materials, as mentioned below:

Covalent linkages are commenced in 2D materials, which harbour surface functional groups such as MXenes, graphene oxide, etc., as covalent bonds developed with suitable agents, can only be effective in inducing functionalisation on their surface and enhancing the performance. For revolutionary breakthroughs in developing cutting-edge devices, 2D materials through covalent functionalisation with various organic NMs show promising results, particularly in energy storage and sensing fields. The functional groups on the 2D material surface conjugated with ligands through covalent bonding facilitate the functionalisation process. This involves a reduction in dissipation of charge area with subsequent reduction of structural defects achieved through covalent functionalisation. Various NMs, including carbon allotropes, metal oxides, metal nanoparticles, and semiconductors, have been shown to widen interlayer gaps with covalent linkages between aryl diazonium and surface functional groups of NM, particularly MXene-derived materials [94]. This drastically enhances the biomedical and electrochemical attributes of the materials besides conferring protection.

Non-covalent interactions involve van der Waals, ionic and hydrophobic forces. Surface functionalisation mediated through non-covalent interactions is common for carbon-based 2D nanomaterials such as graphene [95]. This type of modification is prevalent in materials lacking surface functional groups and whose structural and functional attributes, such as electrical and thermal conductivity, may be damaged due to covalent bonding. Non-covalent linkages are highly impactful in transforming the nature of NMs, i.e., converting hydrophobic to hydrophilic or vice-versa without any change in the properties of the material [96]. Further, it is observed that non-covalent interactions, driven by van der Waals forces, π - π effect, and hydrophobic interactions, elevate the interfacial tuning of 2D nanofillers by modulating their physicochemical properties. These nanofillers are of utmost importance in emerging applications related to the field of environmental sciences, biomedicine, catalysis, biosensors, etc. [95]. Non-covalent modifications possess full preference of the type of material to collaborate with the 2D NMs as the ally of both the interacting components maintains their properties due to non-diffusion of atoms between them as they are connected with weak forces through physisorption. Organic molecules integrating with 2D materials through non-covalent interactions enhance the output of the devices as the outstanding qualities of both materials outshine [97]. Likewise, the interaction between inorganic components and 2D materials without the sharing of electrons, thus involving a direct interface between them through non-covalent forces, improves their functionalisation. For instance, spiking graphene with small inorganic molecules significantly boosts its capacitance [95].

Band structure engineering is the basis for modern electronics, and modifications in electronic output greatly depend upon the introduction of structural engineering. 2D materials have been reported to be modified through irradiation with helium ions, and for developing patterned defects, a focused ion beam was used [98]. One of the most prevalent 2D materials, i.e., graphene, whose properties are exhibited according to its layer arrangement and structure, shows outstanding attributes in tuning with defect creation involving various groups [99]. Further, black phosphorus functionalised through defect introduction exhibited outstanding optical properties [100]. Likewise, the extensive creation of defects and flaws at macroscopic and atomic levels can easily lodged by MXenes, which tremendously enhances their electronic attributes [101].

It is another influential way for structural modifications of 2D materials to enhance the output efficiency of 2D material-based devices. Structural engineering includes the development of heterostructures, phase modification, and interlayer and pore engineering. These interlayer functionalisations are further elaborated as enhancing the gap between the interlayers, inducing chemical modifications in the gaps and creating a passage. Hole engineering involves the creation of porous layered materials, which have a prominent impact on their properties [102]. As certain biosensing mechanisms are dependent on the optical attributes of 2D materials, their integrating material type on the microfiber side, polished end, or in the mirror, etc., impacts their optical output [103].

2D NMs exhibiting diverse morphologies, surface functionalisation, and compositions develop the best surface for immobilising biomolecules. On immobilisation, there is ample impact on the catalytic output of enzymes as well as the sensitivity of inhibitors [104]. Enzymes are deposited on the 2D materials through a physical adsorption mechanism, either by drop-casting on the NM-derived transducer or integrating them into NMs along with a binder to get a homogenous mixture before depositing it on the electrodes. There has been tremendous improvement in the output of biosensors with the integration of enzymes with 2D NMs, as it aggravates direct electron transfer (DET) from enzymes to electrodes as well as enhances the tendency of enzymes for their substrate [105].

Immobilisation of nucleic acids is facilitated through both covalent and non-covalent interactions such as chemisorption, e.g., gold NPs linking with thiolated aptamers, integration of DNA probe with complementary strand fixed on the electrode surface through hybridisation, biotin-avidin affinity, e.g., affinity existing between biotinylated aptamer to streptavidin-altered graphene-derived electrode, coordinative bondage, e.g., held between DNA harboured phosphate group and metal-organic framework (MOF) attached zirconium hydroxide group, adsorption process as on graphene, π - π interaction, cross-linkages, covalent bonding existing between carbodiimide in modified DNA to carboxyl functional groups in NMs. etc. [105].

Antibodies are immobilised onto the surface of 2D materials either via physical adsorption or involve covalent linkages. For instance, chemisorption occurs between the amine terminal group in antibodies to the graphene oxide-held carboxyl group [106]. Likewise, aryl diazonium salt present on graphene-derived screen-printed electrodes (SPE), when electrochemically reduced, forms the carboxyphenyl film on which the chemisorption of antibodies occur [105].

A nanocomposite fabricated with the integration of bismuth molybdate (Bim) with graphene has shown promising results in developing highly efficient electrochemical biosensors for the detection of mercury ions. As molybdate exhibits outstanding sensing attributes and cycling stability, whereas graphene shows the highest grade catalytic activity, a highly flexible electrode was prepared through their composite. An exceptionally high rate of electrocatalytic output owing to marvellous conductivity and mutual interfacial contact. In addition, the Bim/graphene nanocomposite developed numerous defects and active sites on the electrode surface, which further aggravates their efficiency, besides avoiding the agglomeration of the electrocatalyst. Mercury ion detection involves the formation of a stable matrix between Bim/graphene nanocomposite and mercury ions through π - π association. The sensor developed possesses admissible repeatability, high recovery rate, and minute disturbances to other metals due to changes in current [107].

Likewise, 3D (dimensional) sulfur-amended porous reduced graphene oxide (ps-rgo) has been utilised in developing electrochemical sensors. The composite fabricated exhibited superior capacitance due to 3D porous dimensions and elevated doping rate, which enhanced the surface area tremendously. In addition, rGO, being a carbon-based NM, has higher tendency to attract mercury ions as they have a large number of binding sites. The tendency of ps-rGO to selectively bind mercury ions due to the strong affinity of sulfur in a short period makes it a highly efficient and selective electrochemical biosensor. Besides, they exhibit greater reproducibility as well as extensive storage stability [108].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in environmental monitoring across ecosystems
Heavy metal ions in the soil
ce:section.ce:para: Overview of contamination by heavy metals in the soil: various HMs have been labelled as obnoxious pollutants for the soil environment, viz., chromium (Cr), mercury (Hg), lead (Pb), zinc (Zn), nickel (Ni), cadmium (Cd), arsenic (As) etc. They persist in the soil without getting degraded for ample periods, threatening the life of soil biota as well as human beings. Hence, detection and remediation techniques for these toxic metals have become a global concern. Though various in-situ and ex-situ methodologies have been applied in this regard, the limitations in their application include high expenses, time-consuming, and not eco-friendly. Thus, it is imperative to develop and bring into practice advanced innovative technologies for the detection and remediation of HMs that can be safely employed to protect the soil environment [109].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D material-based electrochemical and optical sensing techniques for HM ions detection in soil
ce:section.ce:para: A study conducted by Ding et al. led to the development of carbon material, viz., CZIFS, referring to a carbon-derived hybrid double-shelled framework, wherein the outer shell is composed of Co-graphitic carbon, and the inner shell is made up of microporous carbon [110]. Thereafter, aptamer was integrated on screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) with CZIFS. CZIFS were involved in signal transduction as well as the amplification process, whereas thionine served as a signal factor. It is observed that this aptasensor exhibited high efficiency, greater selectivity and reproducibility in the detection of lead metal ions from polluted soils [110], [111].

Xi and co-workers prepared thiourea (TU) functionalised cadmium selenide or cadmium sulfide (CdSe/Cds) quantum dots (QDS), which work as fluorescent sensors for sensing mercury ions. The functionalisation of QDS with TU involved electrostatic interaction between them, with simultaneous capping of thioglycolic acid (TGA). The underlying mechanism is described as the TGA-capped QDS on dissolution in phosphate buffer (ph 7.17), leading to deprotonation of the TGA group on QDS, creating a negatively charged surface. On the other hand, TU possessed a nitrogen atom with a positive charge and a sulfur atom with a negative charge due to intramolecular electron movement. Hence, TU-modified QDS are developed due to electrostatic interaction between the positively charged amine group of TU and the negatively charged TGA-capped QDS. At the same time, the remaining exposed sulfur atoms with negative charge interact with the analyte, mercury ion, in this case. The supplementation of mercury ions leads to quenching of the fluorescence rate of the modified QDS, and it depends on the amount of mercury ions. The quenching is facilitated via the interaction of mercury ions and TU-functionalized on the TGA-bonded QDS, causing a decline in fluorescence intensity. The developed fluorescent sensor exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of mercury ions from various polluted soils [112].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metal ions in the water
ce:section.ce:para: Overview of contamination by heavy metals in the water: the water resources get contaminated by heavy metals through various activities, which include industrial discharge, domestic resources, agricultural activities, and natural processes. The excessive amount of toxic metals in the industrial effluents directly or indirectly enters the various ecosystems and ultimately accumulates in the food chain, impacting human beings as well as terrestrial and aquatic animals [113]. Domestic waste, besides containing microbes and organic matter, is also a reservoir of ample amounts of metals, salts, oils and grease. The water of the famous Yamuna River is highly contaminated with domestic discharge [114]. Moreover, the agrochemicals comprising pesticides and insecticides are loaded with toxic metal ion content. The rainfall, especially during the rainy season, plays a major role in the drainage, leaching and surface runoff process, thus transporting all the contaminants to the river water [115]. Customary metal ion detection techniques, viz., X-ray fluorescence, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), and inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), have multiple drawbacks, including bulky instruments that need a trained technician to operate them. Thus, impose limitations for their on-site usage, consuming a lot of time and being heavy on the pocket as well. Thus, advancements in the sensing field led to the development of miniaturisation of the bulky instruments consisting of three electrodes- derived optical and electrochemical biosensors [116].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D Material-based electrochemical and optical sensing techniques for HM ions detection in water
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene in electrochemical sensing of heavy metals in water: graphene as such has limited applications, but it is a gem of a material after integrating with other materials such as metal oxides, metal ions, enzymes, polymers, etc., which tremendously enhances the conductivity, stability, as well as electrocatalytic output. The graphene-based composites have shown promising results in the identification of pollutants, including HMs from the environment [117]. Hence, depending upon the type of pollutant to be detected, the corresponding bio-recognition element, viz., enzymes, aptamers, antibodies, or nucleic acids, are rendered immobilised on the graphene surface. Integration of bio-recognition elements with the 2D graphene-based composites has significantly enhanced the selectivity and sensitivity of the biosensors, making them highly suitable for real-time monitoring of environmental pollutants [118].

Nucleic acid-modified graphene-derived biosensing platforms: biosensors with DNA as a biorecognition element have gained enormous attention for the detection of metal ions from the environment due to their tendency of exclusive interaction with base pairs, association and dissociation of DNA. The carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups of 2D graphene oxide (GO) make them suitable candidates for developing GO-DNA-based biosensors via immobilisation of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) on GO with π π association. A study reported the amendment of gold (Au) electrodes with amino-altered rGO (NH2-rGO), ion-selective DNA, and DNAzyme for the detection of lead and mercury ions [119]. The mechanism involved the functionalisation of GO with the amino group through nitrogen plasma, simultaneously preparing pre-hybridised DNA by using DNA, DNA substrate, and DNAzyme. Thereafter, DNA film was obtained by incubation of NH2-rGO modified Au electrode in pre-hybridised DNA solution for 12 h. Hence, the resulting electrode assembly (Au/NH2-rGO-DNA) was then employed for the identification of Hg and Pb metal ions via an electrochemical biosensing technique.

MXene-nanohybrids in electrochemical sensing of heavy metals: Zhu and coworkers, for the first time, developed a MXene-derived electrochemical sensor for HM ion monitoring. They doped the MXene surface through intercalation of alkali in between the nanosheets, which drastically enhanced the HM ion adsorption tendency by impacting sensing output [120]. When comparing the performance of alkali-doped MXene with only MXene-derived electrodes, it exhibited much higher as well as well-defined peaks. The electrostatic interaction also elevated the electron transport to the negatively charged HM ions. Moreover, for simultaneous voltammetric detection of multiple metal ions, highly defined and widely separated peaks were obtained for each metal ion [121].

Graphene quantum dots in optical sensing of heavy metals: A fluorescence nano-sensor for detection of lead metal ions was developed using graphene quantum dots (GQDS). In this biosensor, GQD served as a fluorophore, and GO worked as an electron acceptor and quenching agent. The synthesis of GO and GQDS involved a graphitic powder oxidation process, whereas the obtained GQDS, when reduced with the application of an ample amount of sodium borohydride, resulted in the formation of rGQDs (reduced GQDS). Further, integration of GQDS to the GO surface is facilitated through electrostatic attraction and π- π interaction, thus conducting the process of fluorescence. On supplementation of lead metal ions to the aptamer-rGQDs probe, there was a rapid recovery in fluorescence prompted by the integration of aptamer-rGQDs and lead metal ions. Qian and co-workers obtained a linear relationship between lead ion content and fluorescence activity [122].

Guanylated Poly(methacrylamide) functionalised QDS in optical sensing of heavy metals: the underlying principle of fluorometric aptasensor for identification of arsenic metal ions is modifications in the intensity of the fluorescence exhibited by the fabricated mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-CdTe@CdS QDS). Hence, the nanoprobe is composed of three basic components, viz., arsenic ion binding aptamer, CdTe@CdS- based QDS, and guanidinium comprising copolymer. The mechanism of detection involved electrostatic bonding between negatively charged QDS, as well as aptamer and positively charged guanidinium-containing copolymer at various levels of the detection process. The negatively charged QDS are quenched by guanylated poly (methacrylamide) copolymer, thus developing an aptasensor. Further, the working of this sensor is carried out in two ways, viz., absence and presence of arsenic ions. In the absence of arsenic ions, there exists an electrostatic interaction between the phosphate group of the aptamer backbone and the guanidinium component of the guanidinium-containing poly (HPMA-s-GPMA) copolymer. The complex thus formed declines any interaction of QDS with the available polymers. Hence, there is no change in the fluorescence intensity of QDS. On the contrary, supplementation of arsenic ion solution leads to the specific binding of arsenic ions to the aptamer, causing conformational changes. Arsenic metal ions bind to the nucleotide bases via electrostatic and hydrogen bonding. Thus, the free copolymer in the solution leads to quenching of fluorescence, hence declining the intensity with increasing concentration of arsenic ions [123].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D Material-based FET sensing techniques for HM ions detection across different ecosystems (air, water, and soil)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTS) in FET sensing technique for detection of heavy metals: CNTS with ample surface-to-volume ratio work as excellent semiconductors, thus highly preferred for sensitive sensing mechanisms. In addition, their outstanding electron transport attributes induce device miniaturisation ability in them [124]. One of the scientific studies reported the significant involvement of single-walled CNT-based FET sensors for the detection of HM ions with aptamer and metal-oriented DNAzyme as sensing elements. They fabricated CNT-based FET modified with silver-specific RNA-cleaving enzyme, efficient for the detection of silver (Ag) metal ions [125]. The sensing mechanism involves the fragmentation and release of the substrate strand from the RNA base with the attachment of silver metal ions, which alters the structure of DNAzymes and eventually modifies the electrical conduction of the sensor. This Agzyme/SWCNTs-FET sensor exhibited high specificity as well as sensitivity in the identification of silver ions from the substrate.

Graphene-based FET for detection of heavy metals: Graphene-based FET (GFET) have been developed for the detection of mercury ions by Zhang and co-workers. For this, the graphene surface is functionalised with 1-octadecanethiol for the attachment of thiol groups on the graphene surface. It is observed that the pristine graphene is unable to adsorb HMIs selectively; hence, in numerous cases, the functionalization of the graphene channel of GFET sensors is conducted with various functional groups. Among them, amine functionalization is very common, thus, 1-octadecanethiol coating is generally conducted to improve the selectivity of this sensor device to a particular metal ion [126]. For the process of functionalization, 1-octadecanethiol absorption is facilitated on monolayer graphene via spin coating of its chloroform solution; thereafter, thermal annealing of the chips at 60 °c for 30 mins was conducted to support molecular assembly. Through atomic force microscopy (AFM), 1-octadecanethiol nanostrips were evidenced on the graphene surface in an ordered self-assembly facilitated by monocrystalline graphene [127]. As thiol groups exhibit great affinity for Hg ions, the sensing process is carried out by the chelation attraction between the thiol group and mercury ions; thereafter, the induced graphene carriers lead to VDIRAC shift, evidencing attachment of mercury ions with the graphene surface-bound thiol groups. GFET biosensors exhibited high reproducibility [116], [128].

Likewise, DNAzyme probes for HM ion detection, particularly lead and mercury, involve RNA-cleaving with metal ions and affinity for HM ions. Based on this, Wen and co-workers fabricated a DNAzyme/GFET sensor for sensing lead metal ions. Gold NPs facilitated the fastening of lead-based DNAzymes on the graphene surface. This DNAzyme possessed a thiolated substrate strand and an enzyme strand. With supplementation and attachment of lead metal ions, the substrate strand is fragmented by the enzyme strand. These conformational changes in the DNAzymes ultimately impact the electrochemical conductance attributes of the graphene, thus depicting the lead metal ion content in the ecosystem [129].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D Material-based ECL sensing techniques for HM ions detection across different ecosystems (air, water, and soil)
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDS) based ECL sensing technique for detection of heavy metals: A diverse range of QDS, including carbon (CQDS), graphene (GQDS), doped QDS, etc., have been utilised for developing highly sensitive ECL sensors. Graphene exhibits marvellous luminescent properties. Hence, one of the research teams designed an ECL aptasensor employing GQDS as a luminescent component for the identification of cadmium, mercury and lead metal ions with great precision [130]. Likewise, for the detection of a minute quantity of mercury ions in the water substrate with high sensitivity, Li and co-workers integrated GO, CdSe QDS, and dsDNA for fabricating highly precise ECL [131]. Further, to address the cytotoxicity and instability of QDS, Li and his research team encapsulated the carbon nitride QDS with silicon oxide NPs, which tremendously enhanced their stability as well as protected them from external environmental conditions [132], [133].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity, selectivity, and stability of 2D material-based biosensors
Factors affecting sensitivity, selectivity and detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: The extremely high surface-to-volume ratio of 2D materials provides a wonderful platform for elevated molecular interactions, endowing high sensitivity and immensely minute detection limits, that too with extremely low analyte content. In addition, numerous active sites embedded on their surfaces further facilitate their strong affinity with the target analytes and empower the immobilisation of supplementary recognition components, viz., metallic NPs and receptors.
