Besides, the substantial surface chemistry of 2D materials, which can be enriched with surface functionalisation, is a major factor for achieving excellent selectivity in the case of 2D materials-derived sensors. The surface functionalisation can be improved via covalent and non-covalent interactions. Although covalent attachment of functional groups exhibits more reliability in improving the crystalline structure of 2D materials and, consequently, their electrical attributes as well as selectivity, it may alter their electrical features, which negatively impact their sensing characteristics [134]. Non-covalent features of 2D materials are endowed via electrostatic, van der Waals, and π-π interactions, allowing alterations in the surface modifications in a manageable manner [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis with conventional heavy metal ions (HMIs) detection techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional spectroscopic HMI detection techniques are based upon the principle of analysing peaks generated by the analyte on dissipation of electromagnetic radiations of a particular wavelength [135]. The underlying mechanism involves the illumination of the test sample with electromagnetic radiation of a specific wavelength, followed by energy absorption by the molecules from the illuminating source, which subsequently induces the excitation of molecules from lower to higher energy shells, detected by the detectors. Thereafter, creating an absorption spectrum via decoding the absorbed wavelength by the ions/molecules [136]. Several spectroscopic techniques have been explored for HMI detection from different ecosystems, viz., atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy (SERS), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), etc. Though spectroscopic techniques have been employed extensively, they are not a promising solution for persuading environmental remediation. The major challenges in implementing the spectroscopic analysis of HMI detection are linked to their bulk and complicated instrumentation, which can be operated in sophisticated environments. Hence, it delimits the on-site detection of toxic metal ions, which consequently hinders its commercialisation. Moreover, the sophisticated infrastructure and spectroscopic instruments increase the operational cost beyond the limits. Besides, it requires highly expert technicians to carry out the process of HMI detection through spectroscopic techniques [137]. On the contrary, 2D materials-empowered biosensors provide a highly efficient way for on-site detection of HMIs across different ecosystems. Moreover, the biosensing procedures are affordable with less instrumentation cost, user-friendly, do not require expert technicians to carry out the process, and are non-complicated and straightforward. Besides, they exhibit high sensitivity, low detection limits, and robust selectivity [138].

The 2D materials-derived FET biosensors for monitoring water quality enable rapid, real-time, and precise detection of toxins, pollutants, and diseases. These biosensors utilise extraordinary features of 2D materials, viz., TMDS and graphene, for locating even minute quantities of pollutants, HSS and other pathogens, ensuring safe drinking water and environmental sustainability. Besides, the implication of impedance and noise analysis, wet transfer technology, and machine learning (ML) algorithms leads to rationalising the graphene-based FET sensor probes fabrication as well as precisely detecting the malfunctioned devices [139]. This quality check, in turn, elevates the scalability and reliability of 2d-based electronic sensors for HM detection in aqueous medium.

The outstanding properties of 2D materials, viz., distinctive optical attributes, superior electronic conductivity, and high specific surface area, when used for designing the optical sensors it provide a cost-effective, facile, and highly sensitive biosensing platform for detection of environmental pollutants including HMIs [140]. Besides, 2D materials are highly efficient substrates for SERS owing to their affordable cost, simple fabrication, and excellent optical attributes. Hence, 2D materials can replace noble metals, which also exhibit high efficiency, but the challenges linked with their high cost, low stability, and photobleaching and catalytic effects pose major drawbacks for SERS [141].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Case studies on 2D materials-based sensing technologies for detection of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
Role of ALD technique in effective deposition of Tio
2
nanotubes (TNTS) and its impact on sensor’s performance for HMIS detection
: ALD has been recognised as one of the highly rated techniques for attenuating the functionalities of NMs, including 2D NMs. This is the only technique which possesses the ability to uniformly coat extremely porous nanostructures of almost any dimensions. ALD represents a standardised technique for deposition based upon successive self-controlled vapour adsorption of appropriate precursors on surfaces, resulting in desired coatings. Hence, ALD is characterised by self-controlled adsorption, which makes it unique from other deposition technologies. As shown in Fig. 9, ALD coatings facilitate tuning of numerous attributes of TNT layers, such as electrocatalytic and photocatalytic characteristics, disjunction of electron-hole pairs, energy storage, gas sensing, mechanical, thermal, and chemical stability. For sensing applications, ALD can facilitate deposition of functional secondary coatings with attuned thickness for exceptional sensor selectivity and sensitivity [142]. A case study has been discussed below regarding the role of ALD in enhancing the performance of TiON/TiO2 nanotubes for the detection of HMIs.

A hexagonal TiON/TiO2 nanotube has been prepared with the usage of an Al2 O 3 (aluminium oxide) membrane applying the Ni-imprinting technique and thereafter integrated into a potentiometric sensor for detection of lead metal ion [143]. For developing a TiON/TiO2-based membrane for sensing, a hexagonal Ni mould was employed to imprint its shape on the Al foil subjected to the pressure of 10 kn/cm3. Subsequently, electropolishing in a two-electrode cell was conducted with the obtained Al foil involving electrolyte solution of HClO4 and C2H5OH in 1:1 ratio quantity for a time duration of 3 min at 2 °C. Titanium oxide is deposited via atomic layer deposition (ALD) process, and this is repeated for about 300 cycles for ensuring sufficient deposition of TiO2. TiON is synthesised through DC magnetron sputtering ( Fig. 10A). From this study, it is inferred that the ALD technique used for TiO2 deposition conferred various benefits in terms of obtaining superior-quality films, substrate-temperature processing, explicit control over stoichiometry and thickness of the film, reduced defects, outstanding repeatability, and scalability for bulk synthesis [144].

The potentiometric sensor comprises three-electrode cells, wherein the TiON/TiO2 heterostructure works as a sensing/working electrode. Thus, with the ALD technique, the Ti/TiON hexagonal nanotube structure was developed efficiently on a porous Al2O3 membrane. The heterostructure thus developed exhibited a hollow tube dimension of 1.2 μm in length and 345 nm in diameter. The TiO2 deposited on the TiO2/Al2O3 surface induced a highly fibrous texture, a greater number of active sites, as well as elaborated surface area, and powerful light interaction (Fig. 10B). Further, electrostatic interaction between the lead metal ions and the sensor displayed elevated sensitivity, more effectively in light exposure as compared to dark conditions (Fig. 10C). It is observed that the TiON/TiO2 heterostructure sensor displays tremendous elevation in the reduction peaks on exposure to light, when compared to dark conditions. The elevated reduction peaks represent the positive effect of light in embellishing the sensing behaviour. The light exposure to TiON/TiO2 semiconductor induces electron excitation to upper levels via photon energy. This results in deposition of excessive electrons on the surface of this photoelectrode, indicating it to be an efficient electron donor [145, 146]. Besides, the introduction of the TiON layer enhances surface roughness, elevating surface area along with multiple active sites. Hence, the tendency to adsorb analytes increases tremendously. The overall outcome of these mechanisms led to elevated sensitivity of the TiON/TiO2 heterostructure sensor towards lead ion detection. Thus, the outstanding capability of the developed sensor membrane in electrochemical sensing of lead metal ions in the water appeals to its introduction in water quality assessment for timely and precise detection of HM content in water systems and implementing efficient mitigation strategies [143].

In another set-up, Neog and Biswas fabricated a sensing unit consisting of finger-like copper (Cu) electrode drop cast with multilayered tungsten disulfide (WS2) nanosheets, thus integrating the properties of a 2D material with the sensor for the efficient detection of heavy metal ions [147]. For this, bulk WS2 was exfoliated for the preparation of multilayered WS2 nanosheets, and Cu-derived electrodes were obtained via etching of Cu clad with iron chloride (FeCl3). Further, WS2 nanosheets were drop-cast on the finger-like Cu electrodes after dispersion and ultrasonication in double-distilled water for 20 min and kept in the desiccator until dried. Thereafter, both ends of the fabricated electrodes were connected to a 2400© sourcemeter, as shown in Fig. 11A. The analysis of I-V (current-voltage) features of the sensor was done under both situations, i.e., the presence or absence of the metal ions. Further, sensing output was noticed on supplementation of different metal ion solutions to the sensor unit. The analysis of the output indicated a sudden rise in the current of the sensing unit [Fig. 11B (a)] as well as deflection in the current-voltage profile from the normal straight line. The underlying principle in the enhancement of current with metal ions addition involves either charge transfer from ligand (WS2) to metal ions or oxidation of WS2 with the solution carrying metal ions. Amongst various metal ions (As, Ni, Cu, Sn, Hg, Se, Pb, and Zn), Zn and Pb portrayed negative resistance in the positive potential region of the I-V characteristic curve and thus can be distinguished from other metal ions. Based upon the observations linked with variation in the value of current respective to the peak (Cp) and valley point (Cv), it is inferred that (Cp-Cv) values for lead metal ions were nearly 13 times less than the zinc metal ions. Hence, this sensor was more responsive and selective for zinc metal ions [Fig. 10B (b)]. Fig. 11C (a) and (b) represent the I-V characteristic peaks of Zn ions and plotted Cp-Cv values at various interaction times, respectively, at a nine ppb concentration. Thus, this sensing unit is highly effective and selective for the detection of zinc metal ions from the aqueous medium. Moreover, it exhibits extreme stability, as depicted by reversibility and repeatability attributes. Hence, it holds immense promise in related sensing protocols [147].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration with remote sensing and IoT for comprehensive environmental surveillance
ce:section.ce:para: Wireless sensor networks (WSNS) implemented for monitoring air, water, and soil quality represent the integration of internet-of-thing (IoT) with sensing technology. It harbours hundreds or thousands of interlinked microscopic devices, which work in coordination to obtain the desired output. WSNS are capable of monitoring multiple environmental factors simultaneously, such as pollution across the different ecosystems, physiological conditions, and early detection of wildfire, thus fostering advancement in sensor miniaturisation, low-power wireless transmission, and microelectronics [148,149]. This technology has a limitation of being fixed at a particular location. Thus, there is limited area coverage as well as diminished processing and storage capability. The limitation has aggravated a growing interest in developing unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVS) in the last two decades, termed drones, suitable for monitoring environments. With the miniaturisation of sensors, advancement in the integration of sensing technologies, covering common optical cameras to highly advanced multispectral sensors, with UAV platforms opened up pathways for monitoring even the non-accessible remote areas. The ability of this sensing technique to capture environmental data at different spectral ranges leads to differentiation in pollutants and natural materials with high accuracy, as well as the development of 2D/3D maps of areas with high resolution, which even space technologies are unable to capture [150]. Another advancement in the field of technology involves crowdsensing, which refers to exploring the sensing ability of the daily used smart devices such as smartphones, smartwatches, smart bands, etc., to record and analyse environmental-related data with no processing charges [151]. Moreover, the mobility of people carrying these smart devices at different locations, either through buses, taxis, trains, cars, or bicycles, provides an opportunity to cover large areas for pollution monitoring, including air pollution, noise pollution, etc. [152].

In this regard, nanotechnology, including 2D nanomaterials, possesses significant ability in developing strong wireless technology for environmental monitoring. As computation and transmittance in the case of wireless technology are dependent upon environmental conditions, implementing intelligent and innovative nanotechnology for fabricating mobile devices will make it convenient to implant these tools in human networking and conveniently pursue worldwide monitoring and analysis. Moreover, nanotechnology exhibits outstanding sensing effects as well as safety features. Thus, it has become the biggest tool for advancing sensor technology compared to conventional techniques. Carbon nanotubes (2D nanomaterials) will be implemented in mobile phones shortly. Moreover, nanotechnology, in combination with IoT, creates things of nanosize that have tremendous ability to effectively and efficiently communicate with people and other devices. Nano-intelligent things are most likely to be the next feat which will revolutionise the world of sensors, as nano biochips possess the capability to transfer data among themselves, the population or the equipment in general [153].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and limitations in 2D material-based biosensing techniques

ce:section.ce:para: 2D materials-based biosensing techniques have surfaced as a promising technology with extensive and diverse scientific and technical implications. The ability to conduct extraordinarily sensitive detection of heavy metal ions across different ecosystems labels them as crucial devices for the environmental monitoring domain. However, certain challenges and limitations in 2D materials-based biosensing techniques still need to be addressed to achieve an extreme level of precision in this field. The biggest challenge includes the controlled, large-scale production of extremely superior, defect-free 2D materials, including 2D graphitic nitride nanosheets, 2D black phosphorus, layered TMDS, MXenes nanostructures, and metal oxide nanosheets. Besides, there is an urgent need to explore the lattice and electronic structure of these materials to understand the in-depth mechanism of 2D materials-derived biosensors. Moreover, certain materials lack sufficient chemical stability and are easily degraded under varying environmental conditions, thus hugely impacting the electronic output of the devices [154,155]. For designing TMDS-based biosensors, consideration of their atomic composition should be highly prioritised because they can induce cytotoxicity on reaction with external chemical agents. Likewise, MOFS indulged in biosensing are difficult to manage as their structure exhibits a highly complicated molecular arrangement. Thus, it’s become a highly challenging task to develop uniform-sized material in different batches. Thus, evolving fabrication techniques for obtaining uniformity, scalability, and reproducibility are another big limitation. Therefore, optimisation of the 2D material fabrication techniques should be addressed, as numerous complex synthesis techniques are often introduced for preparing 2D materials suitable for biosensing. However, when applied for large-scale production, the process becomes uncontrollable and leads to faulty dimensions, thus impacting the device’s performance. Hence, it will be a major hurdle for industrialisation and real-world application of 2D material-based biosensing techniques for environmental monitoring [154]. Besides, attaining the perfect and desired surface functionalization of 2D NMs is another big challenge as precise integration of biomolecules along with maintaining the material’s integrity is supreme for achieving the specificity and reliability of biosensors [32].

While assessing the environmental and health-specific impact of NMs with advancement in the development of nano biosensors, it is vital to analyse the whole life cycle of NM, starting from its sensor-specific fabrication process up to its disposal mechanism, termed as life cycle assessment (LCA). The foremost important and initial step, i.e., manufacturing of 2D NMs, significantly impacts the health of manufacturers through direct occupational exposure. The major mechanisms involved are inhalation of dust-carrying aerosolised NPs, ingestion, and dermal contact. Once they enter the human body, NPs accumulate excessively in the lungs and respiratory tract and lead to cytotoxicity [156]. Besides, the environmental risks linked with 2D material-based biosensors may originate due to the intentional or unintentional discharge of NPs possessing waste (solid, liquid, or aerosols) into the different ecosystems (air, water, and soil) during their fabrication process [157,158]. Moreover, NM-based waste accumulation in the environment may also emerge due to improper disposal of the used biosensors in the landfills, from which NM-containing leachates may enter the soil profile and water bodies. Hence, more advanced and safe methodologies should be devised for the disposal and recycling of NM-based biosensors [159].

In a rare environmental toxicity-related study, it was observed that excessive exposure to graphene QDS (GQDS) altered the gene expression related to detoxification, inflammation, and reactive oxygen species (ROS)-induced pathways owing to stimulation of transcription factor (AP-1) mediated by ROS generation. This indicates oxidative stress conditions created by elevated GQDs level in aquatic organisms [160].

Further, carbon QDS (CQDS) can absorb solar radiation in the spectrum range that enters the Earth’s surface. This leads to quick photo-bleaching of CQDS, i.e., it loses its distinctive fluorescence signature, especially when sunlight reaches the natural water’s interface. Besides, sunlight exposure may induce minute or large structural deterioration to CQDS [161,162]. However, those CQDS which possess high structural stability will be able to persist in the water bodies without being detected as they are devoid of a fluorescence signature. Besides, the sunlight on reacting with water constituents such as nitrates and natural organic matter (NOM) synthesises hydroxyl ions. Upon interacting with generated hydroxyl ions, persistent CQDS generate various by-products which could persist in the aqueous medium for decades and may impose harmful impacts on this ecosystem [163].

It is recommended to take into consideration the standardised protocols for the usage of 2D materials in various applications, including biosensing, to overcome the toxicity imposed by them. The Organisation of Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and European Committee for Standardisation (CEN) approach for Nano-Objects and their Aggregates and Agglomerates (NOAA) analysis gives a clear and authenticated picture of the industrial and research & development in different occupational exposure situations [164]. The OECD has framed Test Guidelines (TGS) and Guidance Documents (GDS) for more than 60 years for assessing the safety of chemicals, in which nanomaterials are also included. In 2006, the Working Party on Manufactured Nanomaterials (WPMN) was developed, and in 2007, the Testing Guideline Programme (TGP) was established [165,166]. The verification of the testing process for NMS is conducted by the combined expertise of OECD member and non-member countries, along with the safety funding stakeholders for various NMs. Despite such huge efforts in safety regulations, like with the usage of NMs, there still exist lacunae, as it takes a longer duration for approval of cytotoxicity exhibited by various NMs during synthesis and application protocols from OECD [167].

ce:section.ce:section-title: New 2D materials with potential for heavy metal detection
ce:section.ce:para: Besides already discussed nanomaterials, a new class of 2D NMs, viz., Janus Nps (JNPS), have been discovered with exorbitant potential in developing sensors for the detection of biomolecules as well as pollutants from the environment. This emerging 2D material exhibits unique morphology with one of the two surfaces aligned in some different local surroundings or maybe functionalised diversely. Hence, JNPS are anisotropic in dimensions, due to which they exhibit superior versatility and adaptability compared to other 2D materials, besides electrical, magnetic, and optical attributes. In addition, JNPS are amphiphilic. Carbon-derived JNP film can be employed for the development of mechanical sensing as well as actuation technologies, as they own robust conductivity, electro/photothermal, or moisture sensitivity [168]. One of the studies reported that CNT and Au-mesoporous silica JNP-based nanocomposites can be successfully incorporated to fabricate biosensors. Biofunctionalised JNPS have been observed to detect the glucose, dopamine and ascorbic acid content from different matrices.

A study reported highly sensitive, facile, and selective colourimetric detection of lead ions based upon 500 μm Janus-compartmental alginate microbeads [169]. A few years later, a group of researchers observed that the streptavidin-altered retroreflective Janus particles exhibited great precision and selectivity in the detection of Hg ions through optical sensing [170]. Further, Xu and co-workers, via a simple microfluidic synthesis pathway, developed hybrid Au nanorod@Ag polyaniline JNPS. These JNPS were implicated for the identification of Hg ions via SERS sensors, and they exhibited high efficiency and sensitivity owing to robust interaction amongst Hg ions and polyaniline [171,172].

However, studies regarding their application in the identification of HM ion content on a large scale are still lacking. Hence, a focus on developing JNPS-based biosensors for the detection of HM ions can be prioritised as it is a highly superior material with unique sensing abilities [173].

Owing to the high efficiency of JNPS, Janus 2D materials have also been fabricated via supratopic binding, which involves chemical functionalization occurring only on the individual face of the 2D material or on both the faces, yet each side is exhibiting their unique chemical functionalities through antiparallel interactions, similar to JNPS. Though various Janus 2D materials possess immense potential to be integrated as sensors, however its practical implications are still in the early stages [174]. For this, single-layered graphene with structurally settled multicomponent covalent chemical functionalization may provide lucrative opportunities in the field of sensing, catalysis, electronics, and photonics.

Advancements in the fabrication and functionalisation techniques for 2D materials have significant qualitative and quantitative impacts. For on-site applications of 2D materials-based devices such as biosensors, the quality of these materials cannot be compromised along with large-scale production. The mechanical exfoliation fabrication process for various 2D materials, including graphene, synthesises excellent quality material but is unable to produce a bulk amount of uniform dimensions, thus hindering its industrial applications. Hence, advanced modified exfoliation techniques such as liquid exfoliation can be implemented for the large-scale production of nanosheets of uniform dimensions. Moreover, the nanosheets obtained are already present in the liquid phase, which provides them with inherent processability. Therefore, they can be used effortlessly for the development of composite materials, films, as well as coatings, highly reliable for diverse applications [175,176]. In the exfoliation of bulk material to obtain layers, the interlayer attraction is weakened. The ions in the liquid may get absorbed in between the spacing of the layers, followed by layer separation through distress (thermal, ultrasonication, or shear).

Besides previously implied mechanical exfoliation techniques, chemical vapour deposition (CVD) methodologies are used extensively for the mass production of 2D materials. However, for the integration of 2D materials in various devices, further processing is required to optimise the properties. For instance, graphene fabricated through the CVD technique, if required to integrate into silicon-derived devices, should be processed to get rid of useless metal matrices. In this regard, the wet-chemistry process was found to be highly suitable, which involves spin coating of CVD-fabricated graphene with polymer or photoresist material. Multiple polymer coating onto CVD-fabricated graphene remarkably improves its quality. Further, to avoid unnecessary losses in the optoelectronic devices due to extra coverage of the chip with 2D material, oxygen (O2) plasma etching is another rapid technique to obtain the specific pattern of the 2D material on the chip. It takes a few seconds to etch multiple layers of the material with the O2 plasma etching method, thus extensively implied for industrial production of 2D materials [177].

Liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) technique: This fabrication process is of prime importance in obtaining 2D NMs via the exfoliation of layered compounds. There are two basic pathways for conducting LPE, viz., direct (shearing/sonication-assisted) and intercalation of ions. The sonication/shearing-assisted LPE, also termed as solvent-assisted LPE, is a scalable synthesis process which is free from the impact of temperature and moisture. This technique can be explained through the following steps: bulk material, such as TMDS, is immersed in a solvent, followed by its exfoliation using ultrasound/shearing-assistance with manageable power, and via centrifugal technique, the developed thin nanosheets are separated from the supernatant [178].

The ion-intercalation technique involves the phase transformation, for instance, intercalation of TMDS by lithium (Li) ions converts the 2h-phase (trigonal prismatic framework) to the 1t-phase (octahedral framework) due to excessive ionic species. This transition has a significant impact on its electrochemical attributes as the semiconducting nature of the 2h-phase is transformed to metallic nature in the 1T- T-T-T-T-phase, influencing the output of as-synthesised TMDS in electrochemical applications [179].

This solution-based 2D synthesis process is highly attractive owing to bulk 2D NMs production at affordable prices with superior electronic quality as compared to mechanical exfoliation and chemical vapour deposition (CVD) processes (83−86). High content of MoS2 nanosheets has been conducted using LPE, which supports the fabrication of ultra-thin films electrode through various techniques such as ink-jet printing, filtration, and spin-coating [178].

Wet chemical synthesis technique: this technique for the synthesis of 2D materials may over-power exfoliation and CVD fabrication processes. It has the benefit of integrating TMDS with diverse thickness scales ranging from monolayers to multilayers. The obtained materials exhibit superior homogeneity and minor thickness deformities when compared with CVD. The edge over introducing diverse chemical components during the synthesis process, which favours efficient material doping as well as application of ligands for surface smoothening or alterations, makes this process highly attractive. Moreover, the implication of eco-friendly chemical precursors in the wet chemical synthesis process may facilitate green synthesis requirements [180].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration with artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) for data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: AI is an important interface for developing and functionalization of smart sensor networks in connection with IoT vista, influencing applications in diverse sectors, including environmental monitoring. AI plays multifaceted tasks, which include fast processing and analysis of huge chunks of sensing data with simultaneous incorporation of vital functions, viz., noise control, data cleaning, and feature tuning for obtaining useful perceptions.

Machine learning is a core component of AI, which involves algorithms and software integration to analyse the outcomes from a given data set without comprehensive programming. The ML consists of multiple steps for data processing ( Fig. 12). The most important and beginning step is the devising and acquisition of data for creating input to acquire further learning, represented as a factor for the built model. As the learning algorithm is dependent upon a huge amount of data, the first step is very crucial. Thereafter, the next step involves establishing a model, applying a training set and subsequently spotting the most suitable algorithm and the affirmation of the model. This is followed by performance testing of the validated model by processing test data and improving the performance efficiency of the algorithm by tuning the model with more data, multiple features, and revamping parameters [181].

AI and ML optimise the real-time outputs in response to sensor inputs, drastically elevate the energy efficiency, and sufficiently prolong the working life of battery-operated sensors. Thus, by integrating data from multiple sensors, AI facilitates a more wholesome view of different ecosystems, which is highly crucial for managing environmental pollution. Hence, AI-driven power for the effective working of smart sensor networks provides valuable aspects in processing, analysing, and drawing relevant as well as beneficial conclusions from sensor-obtained data. This is highly valuable in disseminating innumerable applications in a diverse number of fields.

Efficient monitoring of heavy metal ions across different ecosystems is imperative for their ecological, life-supporting, and societal values. Toxicity incurred due to HMI accumulation leads to severe ecological imbalances and life-threatening impacts on human health. Emerging trends and technologies for implanting advanced materials in different sectors to have promising outputs have instigated the idea of developing new-generation biosensors in integration with nanomaterials, particularly 2D materials, for monitoring heavy metal ions in the environment. The magnificent properties of 2D materials, including high surface-to-volume ratio, robust mechanical attributes, excellent electrical conductance, great thermal stability, and marvellous surface chemistry, particularly crucial for sensing technology, facilitated the ideas for fabricating 2D nanomaterials-based biosensors. We have analysed the co-occurrence network of 2D materials for environmental remediation, which indicates its low prevalence in the biosensing field as compared to adsorbent and photocatalytic applications. Thus, given the alarmingly high impact of obnoxious heavy metal ions, this article has focused on identifying important 2D nanomaterials and their implication in developing biosensors for HMI detection across different ecosystems. The structural and physicochemical aspects of different 2D nanomaterials relevant for biosensing applications have been elaborated. In addition, different functionalisation strategies have been discussed for obtaining selectively engineered nanostructures required to enhance the selectivity and effectiveness of 2D materials-empowered biosensors. As the main focus for HM remediation using 2D NMs in the current article is biosensing technique, hence the mechanisms of various biosensors categorised based upon the type of transduces as well as bio-receptors have been briefed before describing the fabrication of 2d-driven biosensors. Thereafter, the role of emerging 2D materials such as graphene, MXenes, QDS, and metal oxides has been discussed in the detection of HMIs integrated with electrochemical and optical sensing techniques, along with different case studies. Of late, integration of 2D NMs with WSNS has empowered the wireless technology for robust environmental monitoring, imparting great accuracy with the least effort. Though 2D materials have emerged as a one-stop solution for pollutant removal across different ecosystems, there are certain aspects which still need a lot of effort to attain high precision in sensing technology for HMIs using 2D materials. Thus, technical and material as well as regulatory and framework challenges have been identified and briefed, besides the secondary impacts of 2D NMs on the ecosystem and living beings have also been discussed. Considerably, the future perspectives and emerging trends in the direction of 2D NMs-driven biosensors have been accentuated with special attention towards novel 2D materials and nano-fabrication techniques, as well as integration of emerging technologies such as AI and ML for obtaining more precise data regarding environmental pollutants.
Virat Khanna: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Priyanka Mahajan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.
